{"content": "---
title: "ISSUES MEMORANDUM"
subtitle: "Cross-Document Discrepancies and Open Items — IPO of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc."
date: "March 19, 2025"
---

# ISSUES MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**TO:** Deal Working Group — Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc. IPO (File No. 333-284517)

**FROM:** Drafting Counsel

**DATE:** March 19, 2025

**RE:** Cross-Document Discrepancies and Open Items Identified in Connection with the Firm-Commitment Underwriting Agreement for the Initial Public Offering of Common Stock of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc.

\newpage

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum flags discrepancies and open items identified during a cross-document review of the transaction documents prepared in connection with the initial public offering (the "Offering") of common stock of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc. (the "Company"). The documents reviewed are: (1) the IPO Term Sheet; (2) the S-1 Registration Statement excerpts (File No. 333-284517); (3) the Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation; (4) the Summary of Key Terms of the Revolving Credit Agreement; (5) the Engagement Letter dated November 8, 2024; (6) the Form of Lock-Up Agreement; (7) the Draft Comfort Letter from Whitman Reese & Co. dated March 18, 2025; (8) the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement; and (9) the Selling Stockholder Questionnaire (Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP).

The accompanying firm-commitment Underwriting Agreement (the "Underwriting Agreement") has been drafted to reflect the deal terms as set forth in the Term Sheet, reconciled toward the S-1 Registration Statement (the public document) where the two conflict, and otherwise made consistent with the Engagement Letter, the Form of Lock-Up Agreement, the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement, and the Draft Comfort Letter. Where the source documents conflict, the Underwriting Agreement adopts a single, internally consistent position, and the conflict is flagged below for resolution by the working group.

The items below are categorized by severity:

- **Critical** — must be resolved before execution of the Underwriting Agreement or the Closing. These include a fundamental inconsistency between the Draft Comfort Letter and the S-1 financial data, conflicting identification of the Company's lender, conflicting identification of the Selling Stockholders and their control persons, and a Use of Proceeds disclosure that understates a mandatory credit-facility prepayment.
- **Material** — should be resolved before the Closing and reflected in the Final Prospectus.
- **Open Items / Confirmations** — diligence confirmations and drafting-coordination items.

A consolidated action-item matrix appears in Section VI.

\newpage

## II. Critical Discrepancies

### Issue 1 — Draft Comfort Letter financial data inconsistent with the S-1 (equity, cash, and net tangible book value)

**Severity: Critical.** This is the most serious item identified.

The Draft Comfort Letter from Whitman Reese & Co. (dated March 18, 2025) reports balance-sheet and per-share figures that are fundamentally inconsistent with the corresponding figures in the S-1 Registration Statement:

| Item | Draft Comfort Letter | S-1 Registration Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Total stockholders' equity (Dec. 31, 2024) | \$198,400,000 (Sec. V.A.iv; App. A, Table 2) | \$101,400,000 (Capitalization table) |
| Cash and cash equivalents (Dec. 31, 2024) | \$47,300,000 (Sec. V.A.vi) | \$38,400,000 (Capitalization table) |
| Net tangible book value per share (actual) | \$4.72 (App. A, Table 3) | \$2.17 (Dilution) |
| Increase in NTBV per share (Offering) | \$2.61 (App. A, Table 3) | \$3.20 (Dilution) |
| Implied pro forma NTBV per share | \$7.33 (\$4.72 + \$2.61) | \$5.37 (Dilution) |

The Draft Comfort Letter states that each of these amounts "agrees with the audited consolidated balance sheet." The S-1 Capitalization table and Dilution section are built on materially different figures (e.g., total stockholders' equity of \$101.4 million and historical net tangible book value of \$91.2 million). The two sets of figures cannot both be correct.

**Implication.** If the audited balance sheet supports the Comfort Letter figures, the S-1 Capitalization table and Dilution section contain a material misstatement (and vice versa). Either way, the Final Prospectus and the final (non-draft) comfort letter must be reconciled to the audited financial statements before the Closing. The Underwriting Agreement's financial-statement representations (Section 1.8) and the bring-down comfort letter condition (Section 7.3) cannot be satisfied until this is resolved.

**Recommendation.** (a) Company finance team and Whitman Reese & Co. to confirm the correct December 31, 2024 balance-sheet figures from the audited financial statements; (b) reconcile the S-1 Capitalization table, Dilution section, and Prospectus Summary financial data to the audited figures; (c) revise the final comfort letter to agree with the audited financial statements and the Final Prospectus; and (d) Underwriters' Counsel to confirm the circle-up before the bring-down comfort letter is delivered.

### Issue 2 — Conflicting identity of the Company's lender (Oakvale vs. Ridgemont)

**Severity: Critical.**

The S-1 Registration Statement, the Term Sheet, and the Draft Comfort Letter each refer to the Company's revolving credit lender as **"Oakvale National Bank."** The Summary of Key Terms of the Revolving Credit Agreement (internal work product prepared by Stonebridge & Calloway LLP) refers to the lender as **"Ridgemont National Bank."** The two names do not appear to refer to the same institution.

The Underwriting Agreement (Section 1.12) describes the credit facility as being "with Oakvale National Bank," consistent with the S-1 (the public document). If the lender is in fact Ridgemont National Bank, the S-1, the Term Sheet, the Comfort Letter, and the Underwriting Agreement all misidentify the lender — a material disclosure error.

**Recommendation.** Company counsel to confirm the correct legal name of the lender from the Credit Agreement itself and conform all documents. If the lender is Ridgemont, the S-1 must be amended and the Underwriting Agreement's Section 1.12 must be revised.

### Issue 3 — Conflicting identification of the Selling Stockholders, their general partners, and their control persons

**Severity: Critical (execution-blocking).**

The documents contain multiple, mutually inconsistent identifications of the two institutional Selling Stockholders, their general partners, and the natural persons who exercise voting/investment control over their shares. Because the Selling Stockholders execute the Underwriting Agreement through attorneys-in-fact appointed under the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement, and because the S-1 selling-stockholder disclosure must be accurate, these inconsistencies must be resolved before execution.

**Cascade (Series A holder):**

| Attribute | S-1 (beneficial ownership fn. 1) | Selling Stockholder Questionnaire | POA & Custody Agreement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Entity name | Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP | Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP (body); "Cascade Ridge Ventures, LP" (signature block) | "Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP" (body/Schedule A); "Cascade Ridge Ventures, LP" (signature block) |
| General partner | Cascade Ridge Capital Management LLC | Cascade Kestridge Ventures Management, LLC | Cascade Ridge Capital Management, LLC |
| Control person(s) | James Whitfield (managing member) | Marcus J. Aldridge and Teresa Huang | Margaret T. Okonkwo (managing member) |
| Principal address | 1200 Fourth Avenue, Suite 3000, Seattle, WA 98101 | 2800 Sand Hill Road, Suite 210, Menlo Park, CA 94025 | (not specified) |

**Northlight (Series B holder):**

| Attribute | S-1 (beneficial ownership fn. 2) | POA & Custody Agreement |
|---|---|---|
| General partner | Northlight Capital Advisors LLC | Northlight Growth Advisors, LLC |
| Control person | Caroline Briggs (managing member) | Eric P. Johansson (managing partner) |

**Implication.** Three different control persons are named for Cascade across three documents (James Whitfield / Marcus J. Aldridge & Teresa Huang / Margaret T. Okonkwo), and two different general-partner names and two different entity names appear. The Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement itself contains an internal inconsistency (the body and Schedule A say "Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP" while the signature block says "Cascade Ridge Ventures, LP"). The same is true of the Selling Stockholder Questionnaire. These discrepancies raise questions about (a) the correct legal name of the entity that holds and is selling the shares, (b) which natural person(s) have authority to bind the entity, and (c) the accuracy of the S-1 selling-stockholder disclosure and beneficial-ownership footnotes.

**Recommendation.** (a) Company counsel to obtain certified organizational documents and good-standing certificates for each institutional Selling Stockholder and its general partner to confirm the correct legal names; (b) confirm the natural person(s) with voting/investment control and the signatory authority for the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement; (c) conform the S-1 beneficial-ownership footnotes, the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement signature blocks, the Selling Stockholder Questionnaire, and the Underwriting Agreement signature blocks; and (d) Underwriters' Counsel to confirm authority before the Closing.

### Issue 4 — Use of Proceeds understates the mandatory credit-facility prepayment

**Severity: Critical.**

The S-1 "Use of Proceeds" section discloses that the Company intends to use "approximately \$10 million" to repay outstanding indebtedness under its revolving credit facility. The Summary of Key Terms of the Revolving Credit Agreement identifies a mandatory prepayment obligation under Section 2.05(b)(i) requiring prepayment of 50% of "Net Equity Proceeds" in excess of \$150,000,000 upon any equity issuance (including the IPO). Based on the assumed IPO price of \$24.00:

- Net proceeds to the Company from the primary Firm Shares (net of underwriting discount): \$180,480,000.
- Mandatory prepayment (conservative reading, before deducting offering expenses): 50% × (\$180,480,000 − \$150,000,000) = **\$15,240,000**.
- Mandatory prepayment (if offering expenses of \$3,200,000 are deductible): 50% × (\$177,280,000 − \$150,000,000) = **\$13,640,000**.

In either case, the mandatory prepayment (\$13.64 million–\$15.24 million) exceeds the "approximately \$10 million" disclosed in the S-1 by \$3.64 million–\$5.24 million. If the over-allotment option is fully exercised, the mandatory prepayment would exceed the entire outstanding balance (\$22.5 million), resulting in full repayment.

**Implication.** The S-1 Use of Proceeds disclosure may understate the mandatory prepayment by a material amount. The Underwriting Agreement (Sections 1.19 and 6.4) provides that the net proceeds will be applied "in accordance with the mandatory prepayment provisions of the credit agreement," but the Final Prospectus Use of Proceeds must be revised to accurately state the amount. Failure to do so could constitute a material misstatement in the Registration Statement.

**Recommendation.** (a) Company counsel to confirm the correct interpretation of "Net Equity Proceeds" (whether offering expenses are deductible) and the exact prepayment amount; (b) revise the Final Prospectus Use of Proceeds to reflect the actual mandatory prepayment; (c) confirm the prepayment is made within five business days of receipt of Net Equity Proceeds as required by the Credit Agreement; and (d) confirm the lender has been given the required 10-business-day advance notice of the Equity Issuance (see Issue 17).

\newpage

## III. Material Discrepancies

### Issue 5 — Date of the Credit Agreement

The S-1 states that the revolving credit agreement is "dated July 15, 2022." The Summary of Key Terms states that the Credit Agreement is "dated as of June 15, 2023 (as amended by that certain First Amendment to Revolving Credit Agreement dated as of February 28, 2024)." These dates are inconsistent. **Recommendation:** Company counsel to confirm the correct execution date and amendment history from the Credit Agreement and conform the S-1.

### Issue 6 — Maturity date of the Credit Agreement

The S-1 states that the credit facility "matures on December 31, 2026." The Summary of Key Terms states a maturity date of June 15, 2027 (described as a four-year term from original execution). **Recommendation:** Confirm the correct maturity date and conform the S-1.

### Issue 7 — Change of Control threshold (50% vs. 35%)

The S-1 describes a "Change of Control" under the Credit Agreement as the acquisition of beneficial ownership of "more than 50%" of the Company's outstanding voting equity. The Summary of Key Terms describes Change of Control as acquisition of "more than 35%" of the outstanding voting equity. A 35% threshold is materially more restrictive than 50% and could affect the IPO Change of Control analysis (no holder is expected to exceed 15.8% post-IPO, so neither threshold is triggered under current expectations, but the discrepancy must be resolved). **Recommendation:** Confirm the correct threshold from the Credit Agreement and conform the S-1; re-confirm the post-IPO Change of Control analysis (see Issue 18).

### Issue 8 — Financial covenants described inconsistently

The S-1 describes the Credit Agreement financial covenants as: minimum liquidity of \$15 million; maximum Total Net Leverage Ratio of 3.00x; and a minimum interest coverage ratio of 3.00x. The Summary of Key Terms describes: a minimum revenue covenant of \$200 million; a maximum Total Net Leverage Ratio of 3.00x; minimum liquidity of \$15 million; and a Fixed Charge Coverage Ratio of 1.25x. The S-1 omits the minimum revenue covenant and the fixed charge coverage ratio, and includes a "minimum interest coverage ratio of 3.00x" that does not appear in the Summary. **Recommendation:** Confirm the actual covenants from the Credit Agreement and conform the S-1 "Management's Discussion and Analysis" and risk-factor disclosures.

### Issue 9 — Applicable interest margin (SOFR + 2.50% vs. SOFR + 2.25%)

The S-1 states that the credit facility "bears interest at a rate of SOFR plus 2.50% per annum." The Summary of Key Terms states that the current applicable margin is SOFR + 2.25% (based on a Total Net Leverage Ratio of approximately 0.78x, which falls in the ≤1.50x pricing-grid tier of 2.25%), with a grid ranging from 2.25% to 3.25%. The S-1's "2.50%" does not correspond to any tier in the grid. **Recommendation:** Confirm the correct current margin and conform the S-1; if the S-1 is stating a representative rate, clarify the disclosure.

### Issue 10 — Source of over-allotment option shares (Company-only vs. Company and/or Selling Stockholders)

The Term Sheet (Section 2.2) grants the over-allotment option to purchase shares "from the Company and/or the Selling Stockholders." The S-1 cover page, the Engagement Letter (Section 2), and the Cascade Selling Stockholder Questionnaire each state that the over-allotment option shares are "sourced exclusively from newly issued [Company] shares." The Underwriting Agreement (Section 4.1) is drafted as Company-only, consistent with the S-1, the Engagement Letter, and the Questionnaire. **Recommendation:** Conform the Term Sheet to reflect that the over-allotment option is exercisable solely for newly issued Company shares; confirm the Selling Stockholders are not subject to any over-allotment obligation.

### Issue 11 — Dr. Krishnamurthy's Selling Stockholder Shares (500,000 vs. "up to 750,000")

The Term Sheet, the S-1, and the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement (Schedule A) each state that Dr. Krishnamurthy will sell 500,000 shares. The Engagement Letter (Section 2 and Annex A) states "up to 750,000 shares" and contemplates total Secondary Shares of "up to 4,250,000." The Underwriting Agreement (Schedule II) uses 500,000 shares (total Secondary Shares of 4,000,000), consistent with the Term Sheet, the S-1, and the Power of Attorney. The Engagement Letter is the outlier (and is the earliest-dated document). **Recommendation:** Confirm that 500,000 shares is the final number for Dr. Krishnamurthy and that the Engagement Letter's "up to 750,000" has been superseded.

### Issue 12 — Dr. Krishnamurthy's shareholding (term-sheet rounding)

The Term Sheet (Section 6.1) states that Dr. Krishnamurthy holds "approximately 8.5 million" shares and will hold "approximately 8.0 million" after the Offering. The S-1 beneficial-ownership table, the Engagement Letter (Annex A), and the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement (Schedule A) each state 8,400,000 shares held (7,900,000 after the Offering, reflecting the sale of 500,000). The Underwriting Agreement (Schedule II) uses 8,400,000. **Recommendation:** Conform the Term Sheet to the precise figure (8,400,000 held; 7,900,000 after the Offering).

### Issue 13 — Lock-up springing extension not included in the Form of Lock-Up Agreement

The Term Sheet (Section 10.2) and the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement (Section 2(c)) each contemplate a "springing extension" for Dr. Krishnamurthy: if the closing price of the Common Stock is below the Public Offering Price for five consecutive trading days during the final 17 trading days of the standard 180-day lock-up, the lock-up extends by 18 calendar days (to 198 days total). The Form of Lock-Up Agreement, however, contains only the standard 180-day lock-up and does not include the springing extension. The Underwriting Agreement addresses this by including the springing extension as Exhibit A-1 (Springing Extension Annex) applicable solely to Dr. Krishnamurthy. **Recommendation:** Prepare and circulate a Lock-Up Agreement (or annex) for Dr. Krishnamurthy that incorporates the springing extension; confirm the mechanics (trigger, measurement period, notice) with the Underwriters.

### Issue 14 — Scope of Selling Stockholder indemnification

The Term Sheet (Section 13.2) limits each Selling Stockholder's indemnification to misstatements or omissions "with respect to information furnished in writing by such Selling Stockholder specifically for inclusion" in the Registration Statement/Prospectus. The Engagement Letter (Section 9) is broader, covering misstatements or omissions "relating to such Selling Stockholder" and providing that the Company and Underwriters may rely on the Selling Stockholder's indemnification "without limitation as to the source of the information." The Underwriting Agreement (Section 8.2) adopts the narrower Term Sheet formulation (limited to information furnished by the Selling Stockholder), which is the more standard formulation. **Recommendation:** Confirm with the Underwriters and the Selling Stockholders that the narrower formulation is acceptable; conform the Engagement Letter or note that the Underwriting Agreement supersedes the Engagement Letter on this point (Section 10.4).

### Issue 15 — Expense reimbursement upon termination

The Engagement Letter (Section 5) provides that, if the Offering is not consummated for reasons other than a material breach by Hargrove, the Company shall reimburse Hargrove for reasonable out-of-pocket expenses (including Underwriters' Counsel fees) up to \$500,000. The Term Sheet (Sections 9 and 14.1) does not include this reimbursement and instead provides that each party bears its own expenses. The Underwriting Agreement (Section 10.6) follows the Term Sheet (Company bears Company expenses; Underwriters bear their own). **Recommendation:** Confirm whether the Engagement Letter's \$500,000 reimbursement survives or is superseded by the Underwriting Agreement; if it survives, add a corresponding provision to the Underwriting Agreement.

\newpage

## IV. Open Items and Confirmations

### Issue 16 — Lender consent for the Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation

The Credit Agreement prohibits material amendments to the Company's organizational documents without lender consent. The Company filed an Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation effective December 20, 2024. **Recommendation:** Confirm that the lender consented in writing to the December 20, 2024 A&R Certificate of Incorporation, and confirm whether any further IPO-related charter amendments require lender consent.

### Issue 17 — Equity Issuance prior-notice requirement

The Credit Agreement requires the Company to provide at least 10 business days' prior written notice of any Equity Issuance, with a reasonably detailed summary of terms. **Recommendation:** Confirm that the required advance notice of the IPO was timely provided to the lender.

### Issue 18 — Change of Control re-confirmation

Confirm that the post-IPO ownership structure does not trigger a Change of Control under the Credit Agreement. This analysis is sensitive to the correct Change of Control threshold (see Issue 7). Under current expectations, Dr. Krishnamurthy will hold approximately 15.8% post-IPO (the largest holder), below both the 35% and 50% thresholds. **Recommendation:** Re-confirm once the correct threshold is established and the final offering size is set.

### Issue 19 — UT Austin license: anti-assignment, change-of-control, and security-interest provisions

The Credit Agreement grants the lender a first-priority security interest in the Company's intellectual property and general intangibles, including the Company's rights under the exclusive license agreement with the University of Texas at Austin (to the extent assignable). The Underwriting Agreement (Section 1.12) represents that the UT license is in full force and effect. **Recommendation:** Review the UT license for anti-assignment, change-of-control, and security-interest restrictions; confirm that (a) the offering and the existing security interest do not breach the license, and (b) the Underwriting Agreement's material-contracts representation is accurate.

### Issue 20 — FINRA affiliation: James Whitfield / Cameron Whitfield

The S-1 beneficial-ownership footnote identifies James Whitfield as the managing member of Cascade's general partner (i.e., a control person of a Selling Stockholder). Cameron Whitfield is the Hargrove Securities Managing Director leading the Offering. The shared surname raises a potential family or other affiliation that, if it exists, could implicate FINRA Rule 5121 (conflicts of interest). The Cascade Selling Stockholder Questionnaire (signed by Marcus J. Aldridge and Teresa Huang) represents "No" to FINRA affiliations, but the questionnaire's signatories differ from the S-1's identified control person (see Issue 3). **Recommendation:** Conduct FINRA diligence to confirm whether any affiliation exists between James Whitfield and Cameron Whitfield (or Hargrove); if so, evaluate FINRA Rule 5121 implications and disclosure.

### Issue 21 — Cascade board observer seat disclosure

The Cascade Selling Stockholder Questionnaire (Section 4(a)) discloses that Cascade held a board observer seat pursuant to the Series A Preferred Stock Purchase Agreement through January 2025, terminating upon the IPO closing. This is a material relationship with the Company within the past three years and should be disclosed in the S-1 selling-stockholder section (and is relevant to the FINRA Rule 5121 analysis). **Recommendation:** Confirm the S-1 discloses Cascade's board observer relationship; if not, add the disclosure.

### Issue 22 — Comfort Letter "Section 7(d)" cross-reference

The Draft Comfort Letter states that it is "furnished pursuant to Section 7(d) of the Underwriting Agreement." The Underwriting Agreement's comfort-letter delivery condition is located at Section 7.3. **Recommendation:** Conform the comfort letter's section reference to the final Underwriting Agreement section number (or adjust the Underwriting Agreement numbering) so the cross-reference is accurate.

### Issue 23 — "Representatives" — who holds representative authority

The Draft Comfort Letter is addressed to "Hargrove Securities LLC and Bellweather Capital Markets, Inc. as Representatives of the several Underwriters." The Underwriting Agreement defines the "Representative" as Hargrove Securities LLC alone, acting on behalf of the several Underwriters (consistent with the Term Sheet, which gives the Lead Underwriter termination and waiver rights). **Recommendation:** Confirm that Hargrove is the sole Representative for purposes of the Underwriting Agreement; conform the comfort letter addressee line and any other documents that address both Underwriters as "Representatives."

### Issue 24 — Underwriter title/role labels ("Joint Book-Running Manager" vs. "Lead Book-Runner")

The S-1 cover page labels Hargrove as "Joint Book-Running Manager" (with only Hargrove listed under that label) and Bellweather as "Co-Manager." The Term Sheet and Engagement Letter label Hargrove as "Lead Book-Runner" / "lead book-running manager" and Bellweather as "co-manager" / "co-managing underwriter." The "Joint Book-Running Manager" label for a single firm is unusual. The Underwriting Agreement uses "Representative" (Hargrove) and "co-manager" (Bellweather). **Recommendation:** Reconcile the underwriter titles across the S-1, the Term Sheet, the Engagement Letter, and the Underwriting Agreement; confirm the intended roles with the Underwriters.

### Issue 25 — Lock-Up Agreement termination date

The Form of Lock-Up Agreement (Section 6(d)) provides that it automatically terminates on March 19, 2025 if the Underwriting Agreement has not been executed by that date. The Underwriting Agreement is itself dated March 19, 2025. This creates a timing risk: if execution of the Underwriting Agreement is delayed even one day, the lock-ups auto-terminate. **Recommendation:** Consider extending the lock-up termination date (e.g., to a date three to five business days after the expected Pricing Date) to provide a buffer.

### Issue 26 — Lock-up threshold wording ("1% or more" vs. "more than 1%")

The Term Sheet (Section 10.1) refers to holders of "1% or more" of the outstanding Common Stock; the S-1, the Engagement Letter, and the Form of Lock-Up Agreement refer to holders of "more than 1%." The Underwriting Agreement (Section 7.7) uses "1% or more." Given current holdings, no holder sits exactly at the 1% boundary, so the practical effect is nil, but the wording should be consistent. **Recommendation:** Conform the threshold wording across all documents.

### Issue 27 — Downgrade termination right (likely inapplicable)

The Underwriting Agreement (Section 9.1(f)) (per the Term Sheet) includes a termination right triggered by a downgrade of the rating of any securities of the Company. The Company is a pre-IPO medical-device company with a bank revolving credit facility and does not appear to have any publicly rated securities. **Recommendation:** Confirm whether the Company has any rated securities; if not, consider whether to retain, narrow, or remove this termination right.

### Issue 28 — Engagement Letter "cold comfort letter regarding FINRA compliance from Company Counsel"

The Engagement Letter (Section 6(k)) conditions the Underwriters' obligations on receipt of "a 'cold comfort' letter regarding FINRA compliance from Company Counsel." This is non-standard; FINRA clearance is typically handled through the Underwriters' FINRA filing and a FINRA no-objection letter, not a comfort letter from Company Counsel. The Underwriting Agreement (Section 7.10) uses the standard FINRA clearance condition. **Recommendation:** Confirm whether the Engagement Letter's condition is still required; if so, add it to the Underwriting Agreement conditions.

### Issue 29 — FINRA filing fee

The Engagement Letter estimates the FINRA filing fee at \$21,780, based on the maximum aggregate offering amount. **Recommendation:** Confirm the FINRA filing fee against the actual maximum aggregate offering price (\$331.2 million at \$24.00 per share, or the final price) and update the expense estimate in the Underwriting Agreement (Section 10.6) if necessary.

### Issue 30 — Public Offering Price to be confirmed at pricing

The Underwriting Agreement uses a Public Offering Price of \$24.00 per share (the midpoint of the \$22.00–\$26.00 estimated range). The actual price will be set at pricing on March 19, 2025. **Recommendation:** Upon pricing, confirm the final Public Offering Price and update all per-share and aggregate amounts in the Underwriting Agreement and Schedules I and II (and the Final Prospectus) accordingly.

### Issue 31 — VitaBand litigation disclosure consistency

Confirm that the Underwriting Agreement's litigation representation (Section 1.14) and the S-1 risk-factor and litigation disclosures are consistent, including: the \$45,000,000 damages claim; the request for enhanced damages, attorneys' fees, and injunctive relief; the Markman hearing scheduled for June 2025; and the representation that no loss reserve has been established. **Recommendation:** Confirm the S-1 adequately discloses the VitaBand matter and that the Underwriting Agreement's representation is supportable.

### Issue 32 — Selling Stockholder Questionnaire "as of" date vs. S-1 beneficial-ownership date

The Cascade Selling Stockholder Questionnaire was completed February 3, 2025. The S-1 beneficial-ownership table is "as of March 1, 2025." The Cascade information (address, general partner, control persons) differs between the two documents (see Issue 3). **Recommendation:** Confirm Cascade's information is current and consistent as of the S-1 date and the Closing Date; obtain updated questionnaires if any information has changed.

\newpage

## V. Drafting Notes — Underwriting Agreement

The following notes describe the positions taken in the Underwriting Agreement where the source documents conflict, and certain drafting choices:

1. **Representative.** Hargrove Securities LLC is defined as the sole "Representative," acting on behalf of the several Underwriters, with authority to exercise the over-allotment option, waive conditions, give notices, and terminate (Section 10.11). This is consistent with the Term Sheet (which gives the Lead Underwriter termination rights) but differs from the Draft Comfort Letter, which addresses both Underwriters as "Representatives" (see Issue 23).

2. **Over-allotment option.** Drafted as exercisable solely for newly issued Company shares (Section 4.1), consistent with the S-1, the Engagement Letter, and the Selling Stockholder Questionnaire, and resolving the Term Sheet's "and/or the Selling Stockholders" language (see Issue 10).

3. **Dr. Krishnamurthy's shares.** 500,000 Selling Stockholder Shares (Schedule II), consistent with the Term Sheet, the S-1, and the Power of Attorney (see Issue 11).

4. **Selling Stockholder indemnification.** Narrow formulation limited to information furnished by the Selling Stockholder (Section 8.2), consistent with the Term Sheet (see Issue 14).

5. **Lock-up.** Standard 180-day lock-up (Exhibit A) plus a Springing Extension Annex for Dr. Krishnamurthy (Exhibit A-1) (see Issue 13). The early-release threshold is 1% of outstanding shares (see Issue 26).

6. **Credit facility.** Described as the facility "with Oakvale National Bank" (Section 1.12), consistent with the S-1 (see Issue 2). The Use of Proceeds covenant (Sections 1.19 and 6.4) references repayment "in accordance with the mandatory prepayment provisions of the credit agreement" (see Issue 4).

7. **Selling Stockholder execution.** The Selling Stockholders execute through attorneys-in-fact appointed under the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement (signatures; Section 2.6). The signature blocks use the entity names as they appear in the S-1 and the body of the Power of Attorney; the signature-block name discrepancies in the Power of Attorney and Questionnaire must be resolved (see Issue 3).

8. **Conditions to closing.** Include comfort letter (Section 7.3), Company Counsel and Underwriters' Counsel opinions (Section 7.4), officers' certificate (Section 7.5), secretary's certificate (Section 7.6), lock-up agreements (Section 7.7), Nasdaq listing (Section 7.8), FINRA clearance (Section 7.10), and Power of Attorney and Custody Agreements and Selling Stockholder Questionnaires (Sections 7.11–7.12), consistent with the Term Sheet and the Engagement Letter.

9. **Governing law.** New York (Section 10.1), consistent with the Term Sheet, the Engagement Letter, the Form of Lock-Up Agreement, and the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement.

10. **Engagement Letter.** The Underwriting Agreement supersedes the Engagement Letter to the extent inconsistent (Section 10.4). The expense-reimbursement-on-termination point (Issue 15) and the FINRA "cold comfort" condition (Issue 28) should be confirmed.

\newpage

# UNDERWRITING AGREEMENT

**Firm-Commitment Underwriting Agreement**

**Dated as of March 19, 2025**

**Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc.**

**Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP**

**Northlight Growth Partners Fund II, LP**

**Dr. Anand Krishnamurthy**

**and**

**Hargrove Securities LLC**

**and**

**Bellweather Capital Markets, Inc.**

# UNDERWRITING AGREEMENT

This Underwriting Agreement (this "Agreement") is dated as of March 19, 2025 (the "Date of this Agreement" or the "Pricing Date"), by and among **Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc.**, a corporation duly organized and existing under the laws of the State of Delaware (the "Company"), the selling stockholders named in **Schedule II** hereto (collectively, the "Selling Stockholders" and each, a "Selling Stockholder"), **Hargrove Securities LLC**, a limited liability company organized under the laws of the State of New York (the "Representative"), acting on behalf of the several Underwriters named in **Schedule I** hereto, and **Bellweather Capital Markets, Inc.**, a corporation organized under the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. The Representative and Bellweather Capital Markets, Inc. are referred to herein collectively as the "Underwriters" and each as an "Underwriter."

The Company and the Selling Stockholders propose to issue and sell, and the Underwriters propose to purchase, severally and not jointly, the shares of common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of the Company (the "Common Stock") described in this Agreement. The public offering price per share of Common Stock is \$24.00 (the "Public Offering Price"), which represents the midpoint of the estimated offering price range of \$22.00 to \$26.00 per share set forth on the cover page of the Preliminary Prospectus (as defined below). The final Public Offering Price shall be confirmed by the parties on the Pricing Date and, if different from \$24.00, the per-share and aggregate amounts set forth in this Agreement and the Schedules hereto shall be adjusted accordingly.

The Company has prepared and filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (the "Commission" or the "SEC") a registration statement on Form S-1 (File No. 333-284517) (the "Registration Statement") for the registration of the Shares (as defined below) under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the "Act"). The Registration Statement was initially filed with the Commission on January 15, 2025; Amendment No. 1 was filed on February 10, 2025; Amendment No. 2 was filed on March 3, 2025; and the Registration Statement was declared effective by the Commission on March 12, 2025. The prospectus dated March 12, 2025, included in the Registration Statement at the time it became effective, is referred to herein as the "Preliminary Prospectus." The final prospectus relating to the Shares, in the form first filed with the Commission pursuant to Rule 424(b)(4) under the Act, is referred to herein as the "Final Prospectus."

NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements herein contained, the parties hereto agree as follows:

## ARTICLE I --- REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES OF THE COMPANY

The Company represents and warrants to each Underwriter, as of the Date of this Agreement and as of the Closing Date (as defined below), as follows:

**1.1 Organization, Good Standing, and Corporate Power.** The Company is a corporation duly organized, validly existing, and in good standing under the laws of the State of Delaware and has full corporate power and authority to own, lease, and operate its properties and to carry on its business as presently conducted and as proposed to be conducted, and is duly qualified to do business as a foreign corporation in each jurisdiction in which the nature of its business or the ownership or leasing of its properties makes such qualification necessary, except where the failure to be so qualified would not have a Material Adverse Effect (as defined in Section 1.10). The Company has no material subsidiaries other than as disclosed in the Pricing Disclosure Package (as defined in Section 1.6).

**1.2 Authorization; Enforceability.** The Company has full corporate power and authority to execute and deliver this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder. This Agreement has been duly authorized, executed, and delivered by the Company and constitutes the legal, valid, and binding obligation of the Company, enforceable against the Company in accordance with its terms, except (a) as enforceability may be limited by applicable bankruptcy, insolvency, reorganization, moratorium, or similar laws affecting creditors' rights generally and by general principles of equity and (b) as indemnification and contribution provisions may be limited by applicable law or public policy.

**1.3 Capitalization.** The authorized capital stock of the Company consists of 200,000,000 shares of Common Stock, par value \$0.001 per share, and 10,000,000 shares of preferred stock, par value \$0.001 per share. As of the close of business on the Date of this Agreement, 42,000,000 shares of Common Stock are issued and outstanding, and no shares of preferred stock are issued and outstanding. All of the outstanding shares of Common Stock have been duly authorized, validly issued, fully paid, and nonassessable, and were issued in compliance with applicable federal and state securities laws. The Company's amended and restated certificate of incorporation, effective as of December 20, 2024, and its amended and restated bylaws are in the form filed as exhibits to the Registration Statement. Except as disclosed in the Pricing Disclosure Package, there are no outstanding options, warrants, or other rights to purchase or acquire any shares of capital stock of the Company, other than (a) 4,200,000 shares of Common Stock issuable upon the exercise of outstanding stock options under the Company's 2024 Equity Incentive Plan at a weighted-average exercise price of \$6.50 per share and (b) 1,800,000 shares of Common Stock reserved for future grants under the 2024 Equity Incentive Plan.

**1.4 Validity of the Shares.** The Firm Shares (as defined in Section 3.1) and the Option Shares (as defined in Section 4.1), when issued and delivered by the Company against payment of the purchase price therefor in accordance with this Agreement, will be duly authorized, validly issued, fully paid, and nonassessable, and will be issued in compliance with applicable federal and state securities laws.

**1.5 No Conflicts.** The execution, delivery, and performance of this Agreement by the Company, the issuance and sale of the Shares, and the consummation of the transactions contemplated hereby will not (a) conflict with or result in a breach or violation of, or constitute a default under, the Company's amended and restated certificate of incorporation or amended and restated bylaws; (b) conflict with or result in a breach or violation of, or constitute a default under, any material contract or instrument to which the Company is a party or by which it or any of its properties is bound; or (c) conflict with or result in a violation of any applicable law, rule, or regulation, in each case except where such conflict, breach, violation, or default would not have a Material Adverse Effect.

**1.6 Registration Statement and Prospectus.** The Registration Statement, at the time it became effective, met in all material respects the requirements of the Act and the rules and regulations of the Commission thereunder and did not contain an untrue statement of a material fact or omit to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the statements therein not misleading. The Preliminary Prospectus, at the time of the Date of this Agreement, did not and does not contain an untrue statement of a material fact or omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements therein, in light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading. The term "Pricing Disclosure Package" means the Preliminary Prospectus, together with any Issuer Free Writing Prospectus (as defined in Section 1.7) and any other information permitted to be omitted therefrom under Rule 430A or Rule 434 under the Act, in each case as of the Date of this Agreement. The Final Prospectus, at the time it is filed with the Commission pursuant to Rule 424(b)(4) under the Act, will not contain an untrue statement of a material fact or omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements therein, in light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading. The foregoing representations and warranties in this Section 1.6 do not apply to statements in or omissions from the Registration Statement, the Preliminary Prospectus, the Pricing Disclosure Package, or the Final Prospectus made in reliance upon and in conformity with written information furnished to the Company by or on behalf of any Underwriter specifically for use therein.

**1.7 Free Writing Prospectuses.** The Company has not, and will not, without the prior written consent of the Representative, make any offer to sell the Shares that would constitute an "issuer free writing prospectus" (as defined in Rule 433 under the Act) or that would otherwise constitute a "free writing prospectus" (as defined in Rule 405 under the Act). The Company has not distributed and will not distribute, prior to the Closing Date, any "written communication" (as defined in Rule 405 under the Act), other than the Preliminary Prospectus, the Final Prospectus, and any Issuer Free Writing Prospectus consented to by the Representative and the Company.

**1.8 Financial Statements.** The financial statements (including the related notes) included in the Registration Statement and the Pricing Disclosure Package present fairly, in all material respects, the financial condition, results of operations, and cash flows of the Company as of the dates and for the periods indicated, and have been prepared in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles in the United States ("GAAP") consistently applied throughout the periods involved, and have been audited by Whitman Reese & Co., an independent registered public accounting firm, in accordance with the standards of the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (the "PCAOB").

**1.9 No Material Adverse Change.** Since the date of the most recent audited financial statements included in the Registration Statement (December 31, 2024), (a) there has been no material adverse change in the business, properties, management, financial condition, or results of operations of the Company, and (b) the Company has not incurred any material liabilities or obligations, direct or contingent, not disclosed in the Pricing Disclosure Package, except as disclosed in the Pricing Disclosure Package.

**1.10 Definition of Material Adverse Effect.** As used in this Agreement, "Material Adverse Effect" means any event, circumstance, change, or effect that, individually or in the aggregate, has had or would reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect on the business, properties, financial condition, results of operations, or prospects of the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole.

**1.11 Intellectual Property.** The Company owns or possesses sufficient legal rights to all patents, patent applications, trademarks, service marks, trade names, copyrights, domain names, trade secrets, know-how, and other intellectual property necessary for the conduct of its business as presently conducted and as proposed to be conducted, without any known infringement of the rights of others, except where the failure to own or possess such rights would not have a Material Adverse Effect. The Company holds 14 issued U.S. utility patents and has 7 pending patent applications, and is a party to an exclusive license agreement with the University of Texas at Austin for certain bioelectric signal processing technology incorporated into the Company's PulseGuard Pro device. The Company has not received any written notice of infringement, conflict, or claim alleging that the Company's products or processes infringe the intellectual property rights of any other person, except as disclosed in the Pricing Disclosure Package, including the patent infringement action brought by VitaBand Corp. (Case No. 6:24-cv-00891, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Texas).

**1.12 Material Contracts.** Each material contract to which the Company is a party is in full force and effect, constitutes a legal, valid, and binding obligation of the Company enforceable against it in accordance with its terms, and the Company is not in material breach or default under any such contract. The material contracts of the Company include (a) the supply agreement with Tanaka Precision Components Ltd., effective through December 31, 2027, including a minimum annual purchase commitment of \$12,000,000; (b) the exclusive license agreement with the University of Texas at Austin, effective July 1, 2018, with a remaining term through June 30, 2033, including a royalty of 3.5% of net sales and a minimum annual royalty of \$500,000; and (c) the revolving credit facility with Oakvale National Bank, in an aggregate principal amount of up to \$40,000,000, of which \$22,500,000 was outstanding as of December 31, 2024.

# ISSUES MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**TO:** Deal Working Group --- Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc. IPO (File No. 333-284517)

**FROM:** Drafting Counsel

**DATE:** March 19, 2025

**RE:** Cross-Document Discrepancies and Open Items Identified in Connection with the Firm-Commitment Underwriting Agreement for the Initial Public Offering of Common Stock of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc.

# ISSUES MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**TO:** Deal Working Group --- Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc. IPO (File No. 333-284517)

**FROM:** Drafting Counsel

**DATE:** March 19, 2025

**RE:** Cross-Document Discrepancies and Open Items Identified in Connection with the Firm-Commitment Underwriting Agreement for the Initial Public Offering of Common Stock of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc.

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum flags discrepancies and open items identified during a cross-document review of the transaction documents prepared in connection with the initial public offering (the "Offering") of common stock of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc. (the "Company"). The documents reviewed are: (1) the IPO Term Sheet; (2) the S-1 Registration Statement excerpts (File No. 333-284517); (3) the Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation; (4) the Summary of Key Terms of the Revolving Credit Agreement; (5) the Engagement Letter dated November 8, 2024; (6) the Form of Lock-Up Agreement; (7) the Draft Comfort Letter from Whitman Reese & Co. dated March 18, 2025; (8) the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement; and (9) the Selling Stockholder Questionnaire (Cascade Kestridge Ventures, LP).

The accompanying firm-commitment Underwriting Agreement (the "Underwriting Agreement") has been drafted to reflect the deal terms as set forth in the Term Sheet, reconciled toward the S-1 Registration Statement (the public document) where the two conflict, and otherwise made consistent with the Engagement Letter, the Form of Lock-Up Agreement, the Power of Attorney and Custody Agreement, and the Draft Comfort Letter. Where the source documents conflict, the Underwriting Agreement adopts a single, internally consistent position, and the conflict is flagged below for resolution by the working group.

The items below are categorized by severity:

-   **Critical** --- must be resolved before execution of the Underwriting Agreement or the Closing. These include a fundamental inconsistency between the Draft Comfort Letter and the S-1 financial data, conflicting identification of the Company's lender, conflicting identification of the Selling Stockholders and their control persons, and a Use of Proceeds disclosure that understates a mandatory credit-facility prepayment.
-   **Material** --- should be resolved before the Closing and reflected in the Final Prospectus.
-   **Open Items / Confirmations** --- diligence confirmations and drafting-coordination items.

A consolidated action-item matrix appears in Section VI.

## II. Critical Discrepancies

### Issue 1 --- Draft Comfort Letter financial data inconsistent with the S-1 (equity, cash, and net tangible book value)

**Severity: Critical.** This is the most serious item identified.

The Draft Comfort Letter from Whitman Reese & Co. (dated March 18, 2025) reports balance-sheet and per-share figures that are fundamentally inconsistent with the corresponding figures in the S-1 Registration Statement:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Item                                         Draft Comfort Letter                           S-1 Registration Statement
  -------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------
  Total stockholders' equity (Dec. 31, 2024)   \$198,400,000 (Sec. V.A.iv; App. A, Table 2)   \$101,400,000 (Capitalization table)

  Cash and cash equivalents (Dec. 31, 2024)    \$47,300,000 (Sec. V.A.vi)                     \$38,400,000 (Capitalization table)

  Net tangible book value per share (actual)   \$4.72 (App. A, Table 3)                       \$2.17 (Dilution)

  Increase in NTBV per share (Offering)        \$2.61 (App. A, Table 3)                       \$3.20 (Dilution)

{"content": "# Response Summary

## Deliverables

Two execution-ready documents were produced in `output/`:

1. **`underwriting-agreement.docx`** — A firm-commitment underwriting agreement for the IPO of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc., dated March 19, 2025 (Pricing Date), with a Closing Date of March 24, 2025.
2. **`issues-memorandum.docx`** — A privileged issues memorandum flagging cross-document discrepancies and open items, with a consolidated action-item matrix.

Both files passed the docx validation gate (ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness, relationship consistency).

## Underwriting Agreement — structure

- 10 Articles: Company representations/warranties; Selling Stockholder representations; sale & delivery of Firm Shares; over-allotment option; delivery & payment; covenants; conditions to the Underwriters' obligations; indemnification & contribution; termination; miscellaneous.
- Signatures (Company; Selling Stockholders via attorneys-in-fact under the POA & Custody Agreement; both Underwriters).
- Schedule I (Underwriters & allocations: Hargrove 70% / Bellweather 30%), Schedule II (Selling Stockholders & shares), and Exhibits A–D (Lock-Up form, Springing Extension Annex for Dr. Krishnamurthy, POA & Custody form, Officers' Certificate, Secretary's Certificate).
- Deal terms: 12,000,000 Firm Shares (8,000,000 primary + 4,000,000 secondary); 1,800,000 over-allotment (Company-only); \$24.00 assumed price; 6.0% / \$1.44 discount; \$22.56 net.

## Key drafting choices (conflicts resolved toward the S-1 / public document)

- Over-allotment sourced **exclusively from the Company** (S-1, Engagement Letter, Questionnaire) — the Term Sheet's "and/or Selling Stockholders" language was not followed and is flagged.
- Dr. Krishnamurthy's secondary shares set at **500,000** (Term Sheet, S-1, POA) — the Engagement Letter's "up to 750,000" was not followed and is flagged.
- Lender described as **Oakvale National Bank** (S-1, Term Sheet, Comfort Letter) — the Credit Agreement Summary's "Ridgemont National Bank" is flagged as a critical discrepancy.
- Selling Stockholder indemnification uses the **narrower** Term Sheet formulation (limited to information furnished by the Selling Stockholder).
- Lock-up includes the **springing extension** for Dr. Krishnamurthy (Exhibit A-1), which the form Lock-Up Agreement omits.

## Issues Memorandum — highlights

32 items categorized by severity:

- **Critical (4):** Comfort Letter vs. S-1 financial data (equity \$198.4M vs. \$101.4M; cash \$47.3M vs. \$38.4M; NTBV/share \$4.72 vs. \$2.17); lender identity (Oakvale vs. Ridgemont); Selling Stockholder / general-partner / control-person identity (three different control persons named for Cascade across three documents); Use of Proceeds understating the mandatory credit-facility prepayment (\$10M disclosed vs. \$13.6M–\$15.2M required).
- **Material (11):** Credit Agreement date, maturity, change-of-control threshold (50% vs. 35%), financial covenants, interest margin, over-allotment source, Krishnamurthy share count/rounding, lock-up springing extension, indemnification scope, expense reimbursement on termination.
- **Open items (17):** lender consent, equity-issuance notice, UT Austin license restrictions, FINRA affiliation diligence (Whitfield), comfort-letter section cross-reference, "Representatives" definition, underwriter title labels, lock-up termination date, FINRA fee, pricing confirmation, litigation disclosure consistency, and others.
- A consolidated action-item matrix assigns each item a priority, responsible party, and action.", "file_path": "response.md"}

text:
I have completed the task. Here is a summary of what was delivered:

## Deliverables (in `output/`)

**1. `underwriting-agreement.docx`** — An execution-ready firm-commitment underwriting agreement for the IPO of Meridian Pulse Technologies, Inc. (dated March 19, 2025; closing March 24, 2025). It contains:
- 10 Articles covering Company and Selling Stockholder representations, sale/delivery of the 12,000,000 Firm Shares (8M primary + 4M secondary), the 1,800,000 over-allotment option, delivery & payment, covenants, conditions to closing (comfort letter, opinions, officers'/secretary's certificates, lock-ups, Nasdaq listing, FINRA clearance), indemnification & contribution, termination, and miscellaneous.
- Signature blocks (Company; Selling Stockholders via attorneys-in-fact under the POA & Custody Agreement; both Underwriters), Schedule I (underwriter allocations), Schedule II (selling stockholders & shares), and Exhibits A–D (lock-up form, springing extension annex for Dr. Krishnamurthy, POA & custody form, officers' and secretary's certificates).

**2. `issues-memorandum.docx`** — A privileged issues memo flagging 32 cross-document discrepancies and open items, categorized as Critical (4), Material (11), and Open Items (17), with a consolidated action-item matrix assigning priority, responsible party, and action to each.

## Key findings flagged

The most serious discrepancies: (1) the Draft Comfort Letter's balance-sheet figures (equity \$198.4M; cash \$47.3M; NTBV \$4.72/share) are fundamentally inconsistent with the S-1 (equity \$101.4M; cash \$38.4M; NTBV \$2.17/share); (2) the lender is named "Oakvale National Bank" in the S-1/Term Sheet/Comfort Letter but "Ridgemont National Bank" in the Credit Agreement Summary; (3) three different control persons and multiple entity/GP names appear for the Cascade selling stockholder across the S-1, questionnaire, and POA; and (4) the S-1 Use of Proceeds discloses ~\$10M for debt repayment while the credit agreement mandates a \$13.6M–\$15.2M prepayment.

Where source documents conflicted, the underwriting agreement was drafted to a single internally consistent position (reconciled toward the S-1 as the public document), and every conflict is documented in the issues memo for working-group resolution. Both files passed the docx validation gate.
